---
title: "gllvmTMB vocabulary: a plain-English glossary"
output:
  rmarkdown::html_vignette:
    toc: true
    toc_depth: 3
vignette: >
  %\VignetteIndexEntry{gllvmTMB vocabulary: a plain-English glossary}
  %\VignetteEngine{knitr::rmarkdown}
  %\VignetteEncoding{UTF-8}
---

```{r, include = FALSE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(
  collapse = TRUE,
  comment  = "#>",
  eval     = FALSE
)
```

GLLVMs draw on three traditions (psychometrics, factor analysis,
random-effects mixed models) that each carry their own vocabulary
for the same objects. This article gives a one-stop, plain-English
glossary keyed to the terms used in the other articles. Every
Concepts article cross-links here on first use of the underlined
terms.

The math notation matches the rest of the package:
$\boldsymbol{\Lambda}$ is the loading matrix; $\boldsymbol{\Sigma}$ is
the implied trait covariance; $\boldsymbol{\Psi}$ is the unique-
variance diagonal; $\boldsymbol{Z}$ is the latent-score matrix.

## Core latent-variable terms

**Latent factor (latent variable, latent score, score, $\boldsymbol{Z}$)**
&mdash; An unobserved variable that gllvmTMB *infers* from how the
observed traits move together. Each unit (site, individual,
respondent) has a vector of $d$ latent scores. You don't measure
them directly; the engine reads them off the trait covariance
pattern.
*Math*: $\boldsymbol{Z}$ is the $N \times d$ score matrix; row
$i$ is the latent vector for unit $i$.

**Loading (loadings, weight, factor loading, $\Lambda_{t,k}$)**
&mdash; The strength with which trait $t$ depends on latent factor
$k$. A high $|\Lambda_{t,k}|$ means trait $t$ moves with factor
$k$; a near-zero loading means it doesn't. Loadings are what you
read off the columns of `fit$report$Lambda_B`.
*Math*: $\boldsymbol{\Lambda}$ is the $T \times d$ loading matrix;
$\Lambda_{t,k}$ is the entry in row $t$, column $k$.

**Latent factor model (reduced-rank, low-rank, RR, latent
covariance)** &mdash; The shared part of the trait covariance:
$\boldsymbol{\Lambda} \boldsymbol{\Lambda}^{\top}$. With $d < T$
this is a rank-$d$ approximation to the $T \times T$ trait
covariance &mdash; "shared" because the $d$ latent factors are common
to all traits.

**Unique variance (specific variance, residual variance, idiosyncratic
variance, $\boldsymbol{\Psi}$, $\psi_t$)** &mdash; The trait-specific
part of the variance that the latent factors don't explain. Each
trait has its own diagonal entry $\psi_t$; the matrix
$\boldsymbol{\Psi} = \mathrm{diag}(\psi_1, \ldots, \psi_T)$ is
diagonal by construction. The `unique()` keyword in the formula
estimates this.
*Math*: $\boldsymbol{\Sigma} = \boldsymbol{\Lambda}
\boldsymbol{\Lambda}^{\top} + \boldsymbol{\Psi}$ (factor
decomposition); $\psi_t$ is the $t$-th diagonal of
$\boldsymbol{\Psi}$ and the per-trait derived scalar reported by
`extract_phylo_signal()`.

**Implied trait covariance ($\boldsymbol{\Sigma}$, $\boldsymbol{\Sigma}_{\rm unit}$,
between-trait covariance)** &mdash; The $T \times T$ covariance matrix
that the data imply between the $T$ traits at a given tier
(site/respondent/species). It combines the shared (latent) and
unique parts. This is what `extract_Sigma(fit, level = ...)`
returns. It is **rotation-invariant**, even though the underlying
$\boldsymbol{\Lambda}$ is not.

**Communality ($c_t^2$, $h_t^2$)** &mdash; The fraction of trait
$t$'s total within-tier variance that the shared latent axis
explains: $c_t^2 = (\boldsymbol{\Lambda}\boldsymbol{\Lambda}^{\top})_{tt}
/ \Sigma_{tt}$. Values near 1 mean trait $t$ is dominated by the
shared structure; near 0 means the trait is mostly idiosyncratic
at that tier. Reported by `extract_communality()`.

## Tier and group terms

**Tier (level, axis, group)** &mdash; A grouping factor that gives the
fit one block of covariance. Standard tiers are `unit` (between-site
or between-individual; "B"), `unit_obs` (within-site or
within-individual; "W"), `phy` (between-species via the phylogenetic
correlation matrix), `spatial` (between-site via the SPDE mesh),
and `cluster` (additional crossed grouping factor). Most articles
fit at least the `unit` tier plus optionally one of the structured
tiers.

**Stacked-trait long format** &mdash; The canonical data shape:
one row per `(unit, trait)` observation, with a `value` column
holding the trait reading, a `trait` column holding the trait name
as a factor, and a `unit` column holding the grouping factor. The
formula `value ~ 0 + trait + latent(0 + trait | unit, d = K)` reads
naturally on this shape.

**Wide format / `traits(...)` LHS** &mdash; The companion data
shape: one row per unit, one column per trait. The
`traits(t1, t2, t3) ~ 1 + latent(1 | unit, d = K)` formula recipe
pivots wide to long internally; the engine fits the same long-form
model either way. Most worked examples show both.

## Family / link / residual terms

**Family (response distribution, distribution family)** &mdash; The
likelihood used for one trait (or one row, in mixed-family fits):
Gaussian, binomial, Poisson, Gamma, Beta, ordinal-probit, etc. 15
families are supported (see [Mixed-response
families](mixed-response.html)).

**Link function** &mdash; The transformation that maps the latent
linear predictor $\eta$ to the natural scale of the response.
Gaussian uses identity; binomial uses logit (or probit, or
cloglog); Poisson uses log; Beta uses logit. Per-trait in a mixed-
family fit.

**Link-scale residual (latent-scale residual variance,
distribution-specific residual)** &mdash; The variance the
likelihood adds on the link scale when you back-transform to a
correlation. For a logit-binomial trait it's $\pi^2/3 \approx
3.29$; for an ordinal-probit trait it's 1; for a Gamma trait it's
$\mathrm{trigamma}(\nu)$ where $\nu$ is the shape. Without this
correction, correlations on non-Gaussian traits are inflated.
`extract_correlations(link_residual = "auto")` (the default since
the 2026-05-15 release) adds it automatically.

## Ambiguity and identification

**Rotational ambiguity (rotation-invariant, identifiable up to
rotation)** &mdash; A reduced-rank loading matrix
$\boldsymbol{\Lambda}$ and its rotation
$\boldsymbol{\Lambda}\boldsymbol{Q}$ (with $\boldsymbol{Q}$
orthogonal) imply the *same* $\boldsymbol{\Sigma}$, so the
numerical $\hat{\boldsymbol{\Lambda}}$ is not unique. Reading a
specific loading entry "trait 3 loads $-0.45$ on factor 2" requires
a constraint. Workarounds: pin entries via `lambda_constraint`
([Lambda-constraint](lambda-constraint.html)); use rotation-
invariant accessors (`extract_Sigma`, `extract_communality`); or
apply a post-hoc rotation (varimax).

**Identifiable (well-identified, point-identified)** &mdash; A
parameter is identifiable when the data + model constrain it to a
single value (up to non-rotational sign / permutation). A
spurious-extra-factor model is *not* identifiable in the loadings:
running `check_identifiability()` exposes this by showing a column
of $\boldsymbol{\Lambda}$ with near-zero Procrustes-aligned
residual across simulate-refit replicates.

**Boundary parameter (variance pinned at zero)** &mdash; A variance
component that hits the constraint $\sigma^2 \geq 0$. The
asymptotic Wald CI is unreliable near boundaries; profile-
likelihood CIs are conservative there (one-sided when the lower
bound hits zero). See [Profile-likelihood CIs](profile-likelihood-ci.html).

## Phylogenetic and spatial terms

**Phylogenetic signal ($H^2$, phylogenetic heritability)** &mdash;
The fraction of total trait variance attributable to the
phylogenetic tier:
$H^2_t = \Sigma_{\rm phy, tt} / V_{\eta, t}$. Reported by
`extract_phylo_signal()`. Distinct from communality, which is
within-tier; $H^2$ is between-tier.

**Paired two-Psi (paired decomposition)** &mdash; A phylogenetic
GLLVM that estimates both a phylogenetic-shared axis
$\boldsymbol{\Lambda}_{\rm phy}$ + diagonal
$\boldsymbol{\Psi}_{\rm phy}$, and a non-phylogenetic-shared axis
$\boldsymbol{\Lambda}_{\rm non}$ + diagonal
$\boldsymbol{\Psi}_{\rm non}$. Four pieces total. The canonical
form when the tree is rich.

**Three-piece fallback** &mdash; The simpler model when the paired
form is under-identified on small trees: one shared phylo axis +
one shared non-phylo axis + a single $\boldsymbol{\Psi}$ (not
tier-specific). See [Phylogenetic-gllvm: three-piece fallback]
(phylogenetic-gllvm.html#three-piece-fallback-when-the-paired-form-is-under-identified).

**SPDE / GMRF / mesh** &mdash; The Stochastic Partial Differential
Equation approximation to a continuous spatial Gaussian random
field, discretised on a triangular mesh. Each site is associated
with the mesh nodes nearest to it; the spatial correlation between
sites is the Matern correlation evaluated at the inter-node
distance. The mesh is built once with `make_mesh()` and passed to
`spatial_*()` keywords.

## Inference terms

**Wald CI** &mdash; Symmetric CI on the natural parameter scale
computed from `sd_report` (the inverse of the Hessian at the MLE).
Fast, but unreliable near boundaries and skewed sampling
distributions.

**Profile-likelihood CI** &mdash; CI computed by walking the
profile log-likelihood out from the MLE and reading where it drops
by $\chi^2_1(0.95)/2$. Slower than Wald but respects skewness and
boundaries. The default for ratio extractors. See
[Profile-likelihood CIs](profile-likelihood-ci.html).

**Bootstrap CI (parametric bootstrap)** &mdash; CI computed by
simulating new datasets from the fitted model, refitting, and
taking percentile bounds. Slowest of the three. Use when the
profile fails (multi-modal, joint constraints) or when you need
the full sampling distribution.

**Fisher-z CI (Fisher transform CI)** &mdash; The default CI method
for correlations specifically. Apply $z = \mathrm{atanh}(r)$ (which
is approximately normal), build a symmetric Wald interval on $z$,
back-transform via $\tanh$. Closed-form, bounded by construction,
and well-behaved on $[-1, 1]$.

## See also

- [Choose your model](choose-your-model.html) -- decision tree for
  which model to fit given your data shape.
- [API keyword grid](api-keyword-grid.html) -- the 3x5 covariance
  keyword reference.
- [Covariance vs correlation](covariance-correlation.html) -- when
  to report $\boldsymbol{\Sigma}$ versus its correlation
  $\boldsymbol{R}$.
- [Pitfalls](pitfalls.html) -- common gotchas.
